title: Caramel layer cake
subtitle: A tall brown sugar sponge with salted caramel buttercream
excerpt: Three layers of a brown sugar sponge filled and frosted with a salted caramel Swiss meringue buttercream. The caramel is made from scratch and folded into the meringue butter for a stable, not-too-sweet frosting.
sourceNotes: Caramel layer cake is a Southern American tradition, particularly associated with the American South where caramel frostings have been a cake staple since the late nineteenth century. Early Southern cookbook recipes for caramel cake appear in American household manuals from the 1890s and early 1900s. The Swiss meringue approach to the frosting is a later refinement that produces a more stable, less cloyingly sweet result.

content.text: The Swiss meringue buttercream is flavoured with a dry-method caramel. Cook the caramel separately and let it cool before beating it into the finished buttercream. The caramel stage takes care. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it and seek medical care if needed.

The dry caramel reaches approximately 180°C. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Keep a bowl of cold water within reach and do not leave the pan unattended once the caramel begins to colour.

Heat the 200 g caster sugar in a heavy-bottomed saucepan over medium heat, without stirring, until it melts to a deep amber caramel, around 175 to 180°C on a sugar thermometer. Watch carefully as it moves from hazelnut to amber quickly. Remove from heat immediately when amber. Pour in the warm cream slowly. It will foam violently. Stir in the butter and flaky salt until smooth. Pour into a bowl and allow to cool completely to room temperature. The caramel will thicken as it cools.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease and line three 23 cm round tins. Beat the softened butter, brown sugar, and caster sugar together until fluffy, about 4 minutes. Add the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour and baking powder in alternation with the milk. Add vanilla. Divide between the tins and bake for 28 to 32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool on racks.

Place the egg whites and 250 g caster sugar in the stand mixer bowl set over a pan of simmering water. Stir constantly until the sugar dissolves completely and the mixture reaches 71°C (or until no graininess is felt when rubbed between fingers). Transfer the bowl to the stand mixer and whip on high until the meringue is stiff and glossy and the bowl is cool to the touch, about 10 minutes. Reduce to medium and add the softened butter a few cubes at a time. The mixture may look curdled for a moment; continue beating and it will come together. Once smooth, beat in the cooled caramel sauce a tablespoon at a time.

Stack the three layers with buttercream between each. Apply a thin crumb coat to the outside of the cake and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Apply a final layer of buttercream smoothed with an icing spatula. A drizzle of the reserved caramel sauce over the top is optional but sets the flavour expectation on sight.
